skill.name: memory-systems
skill.description: Design memory architectures for AI agents, including vector stores, knowledge graphs, temporal facts, and context retrieval patterns.
skill.summary: Build persistent memory systems for agents that need continuity, entity tracking, and structured retrieval.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: The static external command, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance matches are largely false positives from Markdown code fences, memory-system terminology, and local graph identifiers. The real concern is that the skill teaches persistent storage of user and entity facts without detailed privacy, consent, retention, or access-control guidance, so publication should include a warning.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Memory Requires Privacy Controls
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends long-term memory that persists indefinitely and stores user preferences, domain knowledge, entities, and reusable patterns. The example memory system stores arbitrary facts with entity and session metadata, but the guidance does not define consent, retention, encryption, or access-control requirements.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited guidance and script clearly support persistent storage of user and entity facts. This is a legitimate agent-memory feature, but it can create privacy risk if deployed without controls.

low_findings.title: MD5 Used for Non-Security Identifiers
low_findings.description: The Python script uses hashlib.md5 to derive local node and edge identifiers from labels, relationship data, and timestamps. This is not used for passwords, signatures, or integrity checks, but UUIDs or a stronger non-colliding identifier scheme would be safer for production data integrity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The md5 calls are directly present in executable Python code. Context shows they generate local object IDs, so the security impact is limited.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations correspond to fenced Python examples in documentation. No shell invocation, subprocess call, or dynamic command execution is present in the cited skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown code fences and Python examples, not shell execution syntax. No command execution API is visible in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance matches occur around entity relationships, source and target graph nodes, UUID imports, and temporal query fields. No host enumeration, environment dumping, network scanning, or credential discovery behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static matches are explainable by graph terminology and identifier generation. The reviewed code does not inspect operating-system, network, or credential state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unprotected Persistent Fact Storage Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample integrated memory system stores free-form facts, entity names, timestamps, and session identifiers in local in-memory structures. Deployments should add data classification, retention limits, deletion workflows, and access controls before storing personal or sensitive data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The storage and retrieval pattern is explicit, but the sample remains local and does not exfiltrate data. Risk depends on how a user adapts the pattern in a real deployment.

content.value_statement: Agents lose continuity when every session starts without useful prior context. This skill helps design layered memory with vector retrieval, graph relationships, temporal facts, and consolidation patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains working, short-term, long-term, entity, and temporal memory layers.
Compares vector stores, knowledge graphs, and temporal knowledge graphs for agent memory.
Provides Python examples for vector retrieval, property graphs, and temporal relationships.
Shows how to retrieve memories by semantic query, entity, and time range.
Describes consolidation patterns for duplicate facts, outdated facts, and index rebuilding.
Connects memory retrieval to context-building workflows for agents.
limitations: Example embeddings are placeholders and are not suitable for production semantic search.
The sample graph implementation is simplified and does not replace a production graph database.
Privacy, consent, retention, and access-control policies must be designed separately.
The skill does not provide hosted storage, model APIs, or a complete deployment framework.

use_cases.title: Add Continuity to a Support Agent
use_cases.description: Design memory that tracks customer entities, preferences, open issues, and resolved facts across sessions.

use_cases.title: Model Changing Business Facts
use_cases.description: Use temporal relationships to answer questions about what was true at a specific date or time range.

use_cases.title: Choose a Memory Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare file memory, vector retrieval, graph memory, and temporal graphs before building an agent platform.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Memory Layer
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a simple agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a memory layer for an agent that must remember user preferences across sessions. Include risks and tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design Entity Memory
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an entity memory model for my agent. Include entity types, relationships, metadata, and retrieval paths.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Temporal Retrieval
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced design for facts that change over time.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a temporal memory design for facts that change over time. Include validity windows, query patterns, and conflict handling.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Memory Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed agent memory architecture for privacy, retrieval quality, data retention, consolidation, and failure modes.

output_examples.input: I need memory for an assistant that remembers customer preferences and purchases.
output: A layered design with session memory for current tickets, entity memory for customers, and long-term storage for stable preferences.
A recommendation to store purchases as relationship facts with timestamps and confidence scores.
A warning to define consent, deletion, and retention rules before saving personal data.

output_examples.input: I need to know which facts were true on a past date.
output: A temporal graph approach with valid-from and valid-until dates on relationships.
Query guidance for retrieving facts that overlap the requested time window.
Conflict handling guidance for superseded or uncertain facts.

output_examples.input: My vector store retrieves related text but misses relationships.
output: A comparison of vector retrieval and graph memory for relationship reasoning.
A migration path that adds entity extraction, relationship indexes, and graph-based retrieval.
A consolidation plan to merge duplicate facts and remove outdated entries.

best_practices: Start with the simplest memory layer that supports the required queries.
Attach source, timestamp, confidence, and validity metadata to stored facts.
Define privacy, retention, deletion, and access-control policies before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not store sensitive user facts indefinitely without consent and deletion workflows.
Do not rely only on vector similarity when relationship reasoning is required.
Do not inject every stored memory into context without relevance filtering.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps design memory systems for agents that need persistent state, entity consistency, and structured retrieval.

faq.question: Does it provide production-ready storage?
faq.answer: No. It provides concepts and simplified Python examples that need production hardening.

faq.question: When should I use a knowledge graph?
faq.answer: Use a knowledge graph when questions depend on relationships between entities, not only text similarity.

faq.question: When do temporal knowledge graphs matter?
faq.answer: They matter when facts change over time and the agent must answer what was true at a specific time.

faq.question: Can this work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is tool-agnostic and can support memory design for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What security concern should users consider first?
faq.answer: Persistent memory can store personal or sensitive data, so consent, retention, deletion, and access controls are essential.
